item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is great. I love the Stop Sign That Terrorizes You. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The author's answered that earlier in the comments: there's no compulsion effect. It doesn't talk, either. It just moves around, anything else are their own emotional reactions to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Does the sign have a secondary anomolous property? It seems like the description should mention something about compulsion - as no normal person would obey a stop sign whispering in their head. Also, could it be pending neutralized since its motivation is gone? (Not sure about that one, depends on your vision for it).

Otherwise a fantastic article. The storytelling was immensely fun to figure out. +1 for sure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I seriously enjoy the way skips have the ability to so cleanly blend the absurd and the serious, and when authors really lean into that, it produces some magic. Should I be laughing at the absurd imagery or gasping at the horror wrought upon this suburban family? The answer is yes, and the vote is +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really interesting story, and a great ending. Makes me feel like the stop sign is somehow connected to Thomas Gregg though. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a pretty good SCP, my dude.
I enjoy how all the weird reactions aren't a direct result of the anomaly - you could've stuck a normal stop sign where it would've appeared and you would've gotten the same results. I think that's really neat.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Congratulations on publishing your first SCP!

However, I did find the description unfinished since it doesn't say anything about the pattern. Plus, the second encounter does feel unnecessary since it doesn't add much to the story, in my opinion. Another thing is that it went to fast? I understand that being your first article, but you could maybe develop a bit more your pacing.

Overall, I did find your article entertaining enough to upvote. So, here it is: +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Heheh…fun…fact…I wasn't aiming for a compulsion effect…I was aiming for a grieving, apathetic wife, an innocent kid, and a mortician who took signs way too literally and I guess I accidentally lead someone to believe it had compulsion effects.

Well. Uh.

This Meta-screw trick is something I didn't know even existed. I could definitely use it in the future.

Well.

Thanks for accidentally teaching me this after I accidentally convinced you that it was diliberate.

Not gonna lie, this double-misunderstanding caught me way off guard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's 'terrible' - perhaps a poor word choice, I was just trying to build on what ManyMeats said - because it looks like it's unfinished. It doesn't even mention the compulsion effect. It's terrible from the perspective of someone who is expecting a normal article. It's a meta screw. It works to your benefit, in my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The first addendum really sold me. The description was ok, I think in a clinical/research environment it would have stated what the pattern is. I think swapping the third and fourth encounter and removing the second addendum would have made the pacing better, but that's me.
Nice read +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea here was very unexpected and fascinating, and it was an enjoyable read.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh?

I'm a little confused on what to do about that…

If it's terrible now, what can I do to improve it without making the article less effective?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think that 'improving' the description, objectively, would make the article less effective.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I see. I'm sorry it ended up being a bust for you.

Hmmmm I'll see if I can 'patch' this story along the way to see if maybe I can improve it further.

Thanks for the time and feedback, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This feels really vignette-y in a way where a lot's being told without much pulling me forward between the addenda. The tone jumps all over the place from log to log and the attempt to ground the narrative at the end with the gravestone reveal felt clumsy and unsurprising. It's a twist which is diluted by being foreshadowed before (poorly, imo: the last paragraph of the kid's note gave it away too early in a sorta obvious and uninspired manner) and which feels emotionally undeveloped.

Were this narrative developed a bit more, and attention given to how the main drama unfolds in an investing way, I could see myself liking this. As it stands, the article's failure to commit to one tone or develop a whole lot to get me invested in the narrative leaves this falling very flat for me.

-1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the feedback!

I knew my first SCP wouldn't be perfect, so I found your more negative take on it to be really interesting.

I don't know if I'll revisit this SCP later down the line, but I will definitely update the Description soon to see if I can improve it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Simple yet effective, and a very fun idea!
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Votes don't help the author, so I've vowed to always leave a comment with mine, at the very least whenever I rate an article negatively. If you feel the same, help out by leaving critique with more of your votes. Comment follows:

I actually got a laugh out of the first log, but from there, the premise outstays its welcome while developing very little. I found the mix of formats and perspectives a little difficult to get into, and found myself becoming uninvested. As presented I think this would work better with just the one log.

A 6 year old would not write long-form prose on tiny lined paper in a perfect cursive font with those inexplicable spelling errors - "gardeen"? I would happily accept an unformatted transcription but if you're going to try to replicate it I want to see you go all the way.

I would have downvoted for that and the terrible description, but then fridge logic happened, to the extent that I'd actually want to see this and the sign's motives developed further. The additional logs don't really do that for me, though, they just emphasise a point that's already been made. Novote overall.
oddly terrifying

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I see what you mean. In my admittedly flimsy defense, it really only has one anomalous property, so I'd say the short Description was a given.

But I can definitely see why it's so lacking.

Any input on how I could improve it?

As well as…advice on the law firm?

EDIT: I did a quick google search for a courthouse and added that it is his profession. I meant for it to be his profession in the first place. Thanks for the catch!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The "Description" of this is really disappointing but it's so short that I can get over it without a lot of hassle. This is kind of…absurdist and winds up being a fun exploration.

Edit: Law firms don't have courtrooms so I am not sure why a judge is chilling at some random law firm.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Done! Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
<3

I 'purrciate it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the text of the letter is a bit too small to read without zooming in. you can fix it by putting a simple
[[size 120%]]Words[[/size]] between the text to make it more easily readable. If the 120% isn't big enough you can always go bigger.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really liked it. Very good execution.
Got some humor in the first situation and also a dark turn farther down. I appreciate the variety.

A stop sign is telling me to stop re-reading and rate it already.
+1

Also congrats on your first skip.

item.title: From Stop to Go
item.content:encoded:
Wow-ee, my very first SCP!

To those who helped me make this change from Stop to Go…

You've been so helpful and patient <3

I still find it ironic how the thing to actually get greenlit was a STOP SIGN!

Many thanks to all the people who helped me along the way:

barredowl

Naveil

chiifu

UraniumEmpire

Riemann

DianaBerry

Grigori Karpin

HarryBlank for helping me format some last second stuff after I posted :)

OCuin

Comrade Waldo

KaraKatt

RockTeethMothEyes and various others in Site19 who helped me find a voice for the kid (seriously I had such a hard time figuring out how to write like a 6 year old.)

And thank you to the peeps who added tags for me!
Srsly thx <3

Edit:

The picture is perfectly legal, because I took it myself :D

Edit: Just so I don't make this an endless list of thanks….

Thank you to everyone who Upvoted :D

For those who Downvoted, I apologize if I did something you disliked and I will do my best on my next SCP to make sure it meets your standards as well as the standards of as much of the community as a whole. I still appreciate the feedback.
